topic: Pick the CNI/network plugin and the Gateway-API / LoadBalancer controller for the 001 k3s-on-Proxmox cluster. Earlier sessions fixed k3s + kube-vip + Proxmox CSI; this session decides the missing two slots.

starting_context.summary: Locked: k3s HA with embedded etcd, kube-vip ARP on vnet0 (VIP=10.0.0.30), Proxmox CSI on data1 lvm-thin, OpenTofu (bpg/proxmox) for VMs, Packer (proxmox-clone) for image bake. Open: CNI choice, Service LoadBalancer controller choice, Gateway API implementation choice.

starting_context.reference: live inspection of BigBertha (2026-07-05, ssh root@10.0.0.1:6022)
starting_context.summary: Host kernel is 7.0.6-2-pve (>= 5.4 needed for Cilium kube-proxy replacement; >=5.8 for full socket-LB). The host does NOT speak BGP; ARP-only for kube-vip is the established choice. Existing k3s-era VMs on vnet0 use q35/virtio-scsi-pci + scsi virtio model.

k3s default Traefik install exposes its LoadBalancer Service via ServiceLB on ports 80 and 443. On a multi-node cluster those ports are reserved by Traefik on every node. Disabling ServiceLB by passing --disable=servicelb is required if a different LB controller is used.

CSI plugin relies on topology.kubernetes.io/region+zone to place PVs onto the right PVE node, and on spec.providerID of the form proxmox://REGION/VMID for VM lookup. Either set these manually via kubectl label, or install the Proxmox Cloud Controller Manager which sets them automatically.

K3s ships an embedded Cloud Controller Manager that hosts ServiceLB. To deploy an external CCM (e.g. Proxmox CCM) you must start all servers with --disable-cloud-controller AND pass --kubelet-arg=cloud-provider=external, otherwise nodes stay tainted and unschedulable.

Both kube-vip ARP and MetalLB L2 have the same single-leader bottleneck for Service LoadBalancers (~3s failover, single-node bandwidth). The gateway-IP-based design from Session 2-3 makes BGP mode infeasible because the upstream gateway (PVE host) does not speak BGP.

tool_invocations.query: k3s ServiceLB klipper-lb Traefik gateway-api external Cloud Controller Manager
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: ServiceLB is hostport-based, consumes node ports when Traefik uses LoadBalancer. Traefik v3 supports Gateway API v1.4 via providers.kubernetesGateway.enabled=true. k3s requires --disable-cloud-controller for any external CCM.

tool_invocations.query: cilium kube-proxy replacement socket-lb kernel requirements
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: Cilium kube-proxy replacement needs kernel 4.19.57+ / 5.1.16+ / 5.2.0+ (best with >=5.3 or >=5.8 for socket-LB host/pod separation). PVE kernel 7.0.x clears all thresholds.

Captured: Cilium provides native Gateway API v1.1.0 (also v1.4 via upstream). Requires gatewayAPI.enabled=true and NodePort/kube-proxy replacement. Host-network mode exposes Gateway on :<port> directly without a LoadBalancer service.

tool_invocations.query: proxmox CCM v0.14.0 topology labels providerID capability
tool_invocations.result_summary: Captured: sergelogvinov/proxmox-ccm v0.14.0 (2 weeks old, 327 stars). Two controllers: cloud-node (sets topology.kubernetes.io/region=cluster-name, topology.kubernetes.io/zone=pve-node-name, instance-type, providerID=proxmox://region/VMID) and cloud-node-lifecycle (cleans up nodes when VMs are deleted). Cluster-aware across multiple Proxmox clusters.
Captured: MetalLB L2 = same single-leader ARP pattern as kube-vip ARP (different upstream names). MetalLB BGP requires a BGP peer and breaks active connections on backend change.

sources.title: k3s networking services (ServiceLB, Traefik, Gateway API, CCM)
Cilium kube-proxy replacement (kernel requirements, kubeProxyReplacement option)
Cilium Gateway API support (v1.1.0 / v1.4 via upstream)
MetalLB layer-2 mode (load balancing behavior, limitations)
MetalLB BGP mode (load balancing behavior, limitations)

pros: Zero install -- ships with k3s
VXLAN needs no L2 underlay assumption (works on routed networks)
cons: No L3 policy / network policy unless k3s netpol controller is left on (it is on by default but uses kube-router netpol which is minimal)
kube-proxy replacement requires installing a sidecar anyway for any meaningful performance
No Gateway API implementation
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Default choice for trivial clusters; for a single-L2 homelab with SDN already wired it's fine but does nothing to consolidate the cluster's networking.

pros: Mature NetworkPolicy and BGP peering (could replace the no-BGP limitation)
Rich eBPF data plane on newer kernels
cons: Requires --flannel-backend=none on k3s and a separate install
No Gatekeeper API without an additional Gateway API implementation (Envoy Gateway / NGINX Gateway Fabric)
Calico autodetection can pick the kube-vip VIP as `InternalIP` -- the Session 2 caveat explicitly warned about this
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Calico brings policy but no Gateway API; with Cilium delivering both, Calico loses on TCO.

pros: Single binary for CNI, kube-proxy replacement, NetworkPolicy, KubePrism GAMMA, and native Gateway API v1.1.0+
Eliminates kube-proxy from the nodes -- IPs autodetect correctly on a single L2
Cilium's Gateway API hostNetwork mode (>=1.16) exposes the Gateway directly on the node without a LoadBalancer service -- plugs neatly into the existing kube-vip VIP
cons: eBPF kernel dependency (cleared at 7.0.6-2-pve)
egress gateway / BGP features still require a router peer (not relevant here)
must set IP_AUTODETECTION_METHOD=kubernetes-internal-ip when sharing a L2 with kube-vip ARP VIP (the Session 2 caveat) -- still required
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Selected. Single dependency for everything networking.

pros: Built in; zero install
cons: Reserves host ports 80/443 for Traefik, conflicts with Traefik LoadBalancer Service
No source IP preservation
Single-leader bottleneck per Service (same as ARP)
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Conflicts with Traefik in the same cluster. Better choice: disable ServiceLB, hand LB to kube-vip.

alternatives_considered.option: Service LB = MetalLB L2 or BGP
pros: Mature; well known
BGP has per-flow ECMP
cons: L2 same single-leader as kube-vip (adds nothing), BGP needs a peer we don't have
extra dependency + an additional IP pool
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: kube-vip already handles control-plane VIP and Service LB. Adding MetalLB duplicates traffic flow.

alternatives_considered.option: Service LB = kube-vip cloud-controller + kube-vip DaemonSet
pros: Already in the design (Session 2 picked kube-vip DaemonSet)
Watch namespaces, automatic LoadBalancer IP from a CIDR
Single binary for control plane VIP and Service LB
cons: Steep install: needs --disable servicelb to avoid conflicts and the kube-vip-ccm Helm release
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Selected. Already chosen in v2; reaffirm with concrete config.

pros: Sets exactly the topology labels the CSI plugin reads
providerID = proxmox://region/VMID matches controller.go's lookup
Replaces what would be manual kubectl label commands at scale
Same author as the CSI plugin
cons: Needs --disable-cloud-controller + --kubelet-arg=cloud-provider=external
Increases moving parts by one deployment
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Selected. Topology labels and providerID are the CSI plugin's contract; automating them is more reliable than manual labelling.

pros: Default in k3s -- no extra installs
Gateway API v1.4 supported when providers.kubernetesGateway.enabled=true
Route to Services via classic kube-proxy / Cilium
cons: For Cilium-only designs it duplicates the L7 proxy
Cilium and Traefik both ship Envoy-derived L7 proxies
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Selected. It's already on the cluster, works fine, and we don't need Cilium GAMMA for cluster-local traffic.

pros: If Cilium is chosen for CNI, GAMMA is essentially free
cons: Premature; off by default and only needed if HA-aware service routing is required
alternatives_considered.reason_rejected: Traefik covers the use case without changing the design.

recommendation:
Set the default stack as: **Cilium (kube-proxy replacement + Gateway API + NetworkPolicy)**, **kube-vip for control-plane VIP and LoadBalancer Service**, **Proxmox CCM (v0.14.0) for topology labels + providerID**, **Traefik v3 (already shipped with k3s) with Gateway API enabled for the user-facing HTTPS ingress**. Concretely:

1. **k3s server flags** (must be identical on every server, identical to the plan from Session 1's k3s install):
   ```
   --cluster-init --tls-san=10.0.0.30
   --flannel-backend=none             # disable built-in Flannel
   --disable-kube-proxy              # hand kube-proxy work to Cilium
   --disable-network-policy          # Cilium handles NetworkPolicy
   --disable-cloud-controller        # run external Proxmox CCM
   --disable=servicelb               # kube-vip handles Service LoadBalancer
   --kubelet-arg=cloud-provider=external
   ```
   Bootstrapping after `--cluster-init` requires Cilium's kube-proxy replacement or a manual cluster DNS workaround. The recommended path on k3s: install Cilium via helm AFTER bootstrap but BEFORE joining agent nodes.

2. **Cilium install (Helm)**:
   ```
   helm install cilium cilium/cilium --version 1.16.x \
     --namespace kube-system \
     --set kubeProxyReplacement=true \
     --set k8sServiceHost=10.0.0.30 \
     --set k8sServicePort=6443 \
     --set ipam.mode=kubernetes \
     --set ipam.operator.clusterPoolIPv4PodCIDRList=10.42.0.0/16 \
     --set ipv4NativeRoutingCIDR=10.0.0.0/8 \
     --set gatewayAPI.enabled=true \
     --set l7Proxy=true \
     --set bgpControlPlane.enabled=false
   ```
   PVE kernel 7.0.x satisfies all kernel requirements.

3. **Proxmox CCM install (Helm)**:
   ```
   helm install proxmox-ccm sergelogvinov/proxmox-cloud-controller-manager \
     --namespace kube-system \
     --set credentials.secret=proxmox-ccm-credentials \
     --set clusters[0].url=https://10.0.0.1:8006/api2/json \
     --set clusters[0].insecure=false \
     --set clusters[0].tokenId=kubernetes-csi@pve!csi \
     --set clusters[0].tokenSecret=<from-secret> \
     --set clusters[0].region=bigbertha
   ```
   Topology labels (region=bigbertha, zone=BigBertha) and providerID (proxmox://bigbertha/<VMID>) appear automatically; CSI plugin reads them directly without manual labelling.

4. **kube-vip ARP** (control-plane VIP + Service LB) — already designed in Session 2. Add the kube-vip cloud-controller (`kube-vip/kube-vip-cloud-controller`) Helm chart to advertise ServiceIPs from a CIDR pool (e.g. `10.0.0.240-250`).

5. **Traefik Gateway API**:
   ```yaml
   # /var/lib/rancher/k3s/server/manifests/k3s-traefik-config.yaml
   apiVersion: helm.cattle.io/v1
   kind: HelmChartConfig
   metadata:
     name: traefik
     namespace: kube-system
   spec:
     valuesContent: |-
       providers:
         kubernetesGateway:
           enabled: true
       ports:
         web:
           redirectTo: websecure
       certificatesresolvers:
         letsencrypt:
           acme:
             email: ops@yourdomain
             storage: /data/letsencrypt/acme.json
             httpChallenge:
               entryPoint: web
           tlsChallenge: false
   ```
   The user-facing ingress is then declared as a `Gateway` + `HTTPRoute`, not the legacy `Ingress` resource. This is the modern, role-oriented path.

6. **Add an OPEN-Tofu variable** to the design so reviewers can flip the stack:
   ```hcl
   variable "cni"            { default = "cilium" }    # cilium | flannel | calico
   variable "lb_provider"    { default = "kube-vip" }  # kube-vip | metallb | klipper-lb
   variable "ccm_provider"   { default = "proxmox" }   # proxmox | none
   variable "gateway_api_impl" { default = "traefik" } # traefik | cilium
   ```
   Defaults above reflect this recommendation.

7. **Smoke test the chosen stack**:
   ```bash
   kubectl -n kube-system exec ds/cilium -- cilium-dbg status | grep KubeProxyReplacement
   kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l k8s-app=proxmox-cloud-controller-manager
   kubectl get nodes -L topology.kubernetes.io/region,topology.kubernetes.io/zone
   kubectl apply -f gateway.yaml && kubectl get gateway -A
   kubectl apply -f vendored/proxmox-csi-plugin/docs/deploy/test-pod-ephemeral.yaml
   ```
rationale: Starting context constrains us to k3s on a single Proxmox host with no BGP peer. (a) Cilium wins on TCO because it covers CNI + kube-proxy replacement + NetworkPolicy + native Gateway API in one binary, the host kernel clears every eBPF threshold, and its single-L2 routing mode matches the vnet0 fabric exactly. (b) kube-vip wins over MetalLB and ServiceLB because it covers both the apiserver VIP (Session 2) and Service LoadBalancer in one DaemonSet with no extra IP pool to manage for control-plane traffic. (c) Proxmox CCM wins over manual labelling because the vendored CSI plugin reads providerID = proxmox://region/VMID and topology.kubernetes.io/{region,zone}; the CCM sets those without per-VM kubectl label commands and is from the same author (single support channel). (d) Traefik's Gateway API support on k3s is essentially free -- the HelmChartConfig flag turns it on -- and it sidesteps having to run a second Gateway implementation alongside Cilium GAMMA.
